module_description: Foundations of integrated circuit design, covering fast adder architectures, CMOS logic behavior, and SRAM memory stability.
title: Adder Architectures & Carry-Lookahead
content_text: CLA units calculate carries in parallel using Generate (G) and Propagate (P) signals to bypass O(n) ripple delay.
quiz_question: What is the primary advantage of a Carry-Lookahead Adder (CLA)?

module_description: Foundations of integrated circuit design, covering fast adder architectures, CMOS logic behavior, and SRAM memory stability.
title: CMOS Inverter Basics
content_text: The CMOS inverter pairs a pMOS pull-up network with an nMOS pull-down network. It features high noise immunity and near-zero static power.
quiz_question: Why does an ideal CMOS inverter draw nearly zero static current at logic 0 and 1?
quiz_options: One transistor is off in each state
correct_answer: One transistor is off in each state

module_description: Foundations of integrated circuit design, covering fast adder architectures, CMOS logic behavior, and SRAM memory stability.
title: Voltage Transfer Curve and Noise Margins
content_text: The VTC shows output changes as input sweeps from 0 to V_DD. Noise margins quantify the disturbance a gate can tolerate.
content_math: NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH},\quad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}
quiz_question: What does a larger noise margin imply in digital CMOS logic?

module_description: Foundations of integrated circuit design, covering fast adder architectures, CMOS logic behavior, and SRAM memory stability.
title: Transmission Gates and Pass Logic
content_text: A transmission gate uses parallel nMOS and pMOS devices to pass both logic 0 and 1 faithfully across the full voltage range.
quiz_question: Why is a CMOS transmission gate better than a lone nMOS pass transistor?

module_description: Foundations of integrated circuit design, covering fast adder architectures, CMOS logic behavior, and SRAM memory stability.
title: 6T SRAM Bitcell Operation
content_text: A standard 6T SRAM cell uses cross-coupled inverters for storage and two access transistors for bitline connection.
quiz_question: What is the role of access transistors in a 6T SRAM cell?
quiz_options: Connect storage to bitlines during wordline assertion
correct_answer: Connect storage to bitlines during wordline assertion

module_description: Foundations of integrated circuit design, covering fast adder architectures, CMOS logic behavior, and SRAM memory stability.
title: SRAM Read Stability and Cell Ratio
content_text: Read stability depends on the size balance between pull-down and access transistors to prevent bit corruption.
quiz_question: Why is a large cell ratio important in 6T SRAM during a read?
quiz_options: Prevents access transistor from disturbing stored 0
correct_answer: Prevents access transistor from disturbing stored 0

module_description: Exploration of dielectric breakdown and the building blocks of power conversion including rectifiers and DC-DC stages.
title: Dielectric Breakdown
content_text: Breakdown occurs when the electric field exceeds dielectric strength. Paschen's Law relates voltage to pressure and distance.
quiz_question: What happens to the dielectric strength of air as the gap decreases?

module_description: Exploration of dielectric breakdown and the building blocks of power conversion including rectifiers and DC-DC stages.
title: Single-Phase Full-Wave Rectifiers
content_text: Full-wave rectifiers use both halves of the AC input, providing higher average DC and lower ripple than half-wave circuits.
quiz_question: What is the main advantage of a full-wave rectifier over a half-wave one?
quiz_options: Higher average output and 2x line frequency ripple
correct_answer: Higher average output and 2x line frequency ripple

module_description: Exploration of dielectric breakdown and the building blocks of power conversion including rectifiers and DC-DC stages.
title: Phase-Controlled Rectifiers
content_text: Thyristors allow DC output control by delaying the firing instant (alpha). This is used in regulated motor drives.
quiz_question: What happens to average DC voltage as alpha increases from 0 toward 90 degrees?

module_description: Exploration of dielectric breakdown and the building blocks of power conversion including rectifiers and DC-DC stages.
title: Buck Converter Fundamentals
content_text: A buck converter steps down DC voltage via rapid switching and inductor smoothing. Duty cycle (D) is the primary control.
quiz_question: For an ideal buck converter, what D is needed to get 5V from 12V?

module_description: Exploration of dielectric breakdown and the building blocks of power conversion including rectifiers and DC-DC stages.
title: Boost Converter Operation
content_text: A boost converter stores energy in an inductor during 'on' time and releases it at a higher voltage during 'off' time.
quiz_question: What is the ideal output of a boost converter with V_in = 10V and D = 0.6?

module_description: Exploration of dielectric breakdown and the building blocks of power conversion including rectifiers and DC-DC stages.
title: PWM Inverters
content_text: Inverters convert DC to AC using Pulse-Width Modulation to shift harmonics to higher, easier-to-filter frequencies.
quiz_question: Why is PWM preferred over simple square-wave inverters?
quiz_options: Better fundamental control and easier filtering
correct_answer: Better fundamental control and easier filtering

module_description: Deep dive into low-level software systems, memory organization, pointers, and manual resource management.
title: Pointer Arithmetic
content_text: In C, incrementing a pointer moves it by the size of the underlying type, not by a single byte.
quiz_question: If 'int *p' is at 0x1000 and sizeof(int) is 4, what is the address of 'p + 2'?

module_description: Deep dive into low-level software systems, memory organization, pointers, and manual resource management.
title: Struct Padding & Alignment
content_text: Compilers insert padding to ensure data is aligned to word boundaries for efficient CPU access.
quiz_question: Why is padding added between a char and an int in a struct?

module_description: Deep dive into low-level software systems, memory organization, pointers, and manual resource management.
title: Dynamic Memory: Heap vs Stack
content_text: The heap provides persistent memory but requires manual management, whereas the stack is handled automatically by function calls.
quiz_question: Which memory area is automatically managed by function calls?

module_description: Deep dive into low-level software systems, memory organization, pointers, and manual resource management.
title: Function Pointers
content_text: Function pointers allow dynamic dispatching and callbacks, enabling plugin-style architectures in C.
quiz_question: What is a primary use for function pointers?

module_description: Deep dive into low-level software systems, memory organization, pointers, and manual resource management.
title: The Sizeof Operator
content_text: The sizeof operator in C returns the size of a variable or data type in bytes at compile time.
quiz_question: What does the sizeof operator return?

module_description: Pipelining, hazard handling, and cache organization in modern processor design.
title: MIPS 5-Stage Pipeline
content_text: MIPS overlaps instructions across IF, ID, EX, MEM, and WB stages to achieve an ideal CPI of 1.
quiz_question: What does the 'IF' stage stand for?

module_description: Pipelining, hazard handling, and cache organization in modern processor design.
title: Data Hazards & Forwarding
content_text: Forwarding allows a result to be used as soon as it is computed in the ALU, bypassing the WB stage.
quiz_question: How does forwarding resolve a data hazard?

module_description: Pipelining, hazard handling, and cache organization in modern processor design.
title: Control Hazards & Branch Prediction
content_text: Control hazards occur when the CPU doesn't know the next instruction address until a branch is resolved.
quiz_question: Which technique reduces control hazard penalties?

module_description: Pipelining, hazard handling, and cache organization in modern processor design.
title: Cache Mapping: Direct vs Associative
content_text: Associativity reduces conflict misses by allowing a memory block to reside in multiple cache locations.
quiz_question: What is the benefit of Set-Associative cache over Direct-Mapped?

module_description: Pipelining, hazard handling, and cache organization in modern processor design.
title: RISC vs CISC Architecture
content_text: RISC focuses on simple, single-cycle instructions to maximize efficiency. MIPS is a classic RISC example.
quiz_question: Which is a core characteristic of RISC?

module_description: Mathematical language of signals, LTI systems, convolution, and Fourier analysis.
title: Continuous vs Discrete Signals
content_text: Signals are functions of time. Discrete-time signals are defined only at sampled instants T_s.
quiz_question: What is the role of T_s in sampling?

module_description: Mathematical language of signals, LTI systems, convolution, and Fourier analysis.
title: System Properties and BIBO Stability
content_text: For LTI systems, BIBO stability is guaranteed if the impulse response is absolutely integrable.
quiz_question: What is sufficient for BIBO stability in CT LTI systems?

module_description: Mathematical language of signals, LTI systems, convolution, and Fourier analysis.
title: Convolution and Impulse Response
content_text: Convolution defines the output of an LTI system given an input and impulse response.
quiz_question: In convolution, what does h(t-tau) represent?

module_description: Mathematical language of signals, LTI systems, convolution, and Fourier analysis.
title: Fourier Transform Intuition
content_text: The Fourier transform decomposes signals into frequencies. Convolution in time becomes multiplication in frequency.
quiz_question: Why use Fourier transforms for LTI systems?

module_description: Mathematical language of signals, LTI systems, convolution, and Fourier analysis.
title: Sampling Theorem and Aliasing
content_text: To perfectly reconstruct a signal, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the maximum signal frequency.
quiz_question: What is the result of sampling below the Nyquist rate?

module_description: Tools for feedback systems design including stability, root locus, and frequency response.
title: Closed-Loop Transfer Functions
content_text: Relates the Laplace output to input. Negative feedback improves accuracy and robustness.
quiz_question: What happens if feedback path H(s) increases?

module_description: Tools for feedback systems design including stability, root locus, and frequency response.
title: Second-Order Time Response
content_text: Governed by damping ratio (zeta) and natural frequency. Zeta determines overshoot and oscillations.
quiz_question: What happens if zeta decreases?

module_description: Tools for feedback systems design including stability, root locus, and frequency response.
title: Routh-Hurwitz Stability Test
content_text: Determines stability without solving the characteristic equation by checking for RHP roots.
quiz_question: For a cubic equation with positive coefficients, what is required for stability?

module_description: Tools for feedback systems design including stability, root locus, and frequency response.
title: Root Locus Design Insight
content_text: Visual design tool showing how closed-loop poles move as gain K is varied.
quiz_question: What is varied when constructing root locus?

module_description: Tools for feedback systems design including stability, root locus, and frequency response.
title: Bode Plots and Stability Margins
content_text: Magnitude and phase vs frequency. Phase margin measures proximity to instability.
quiz_question: What does a larger positive phase margin indicate?

module_description: Boolean minimization, universal gates, state elements, and 8086 architecture fundamentals.
title: Karnaugh Maps for Logic Minimization
content_text: Visual tool to simplify expressions by grouping minterms in powers of two.
quiz_question: Why are larger valid groups preferred in K-maps?

module_description: Boolean minimization, universal gates, state elements, and 8086 architecture fundamentals.
title: Multiplexers as Universal Selectors
content_text: Routes one of several inputs to an output. Can implement any Boolean function.
quiz_question: For a 2:1 MUX, what is Y when S = 1?

module_description: Boolean minimization, universal gates, state elements, and 8086 architecture fundamentals.
title: D Flip-Flops and State Storage
content_text: Edge-triggered elements that capture input at clock edges to coordinate synchronous state updates.
quiz_question: Primary benefit of edge-triggered DFFs?

module_description: Boolean minimization, universal gates, state elements, and 8086 architecture fundamentals.
title: 8086 Architecture and Segmentation
content_text: 16-bit processor using a segmented memory model to address 1MB using 16-bit registers.
quiz_question: What is the physical address for Segment 1234H and Offset 0010H?

module_description: Boolean minimization, universal gates, state elements, and 8086 architecture fundamentals.
title: 8086 Addressing Modes
content_text: Specifies how instructions locate operands using registers, memory, or immediate fields.
quiz_question: In [BX + SI + 08H], what does 08H represent?
